coredata.dc:description: High temperature proton exchange membranes (HTPEMs) with multilayered structures based on carbon dots@metal organic framework (CDs@MOF) and Sulfonated Poly(Ether Ketone) (SPEEK) have been prepared with the spin coating technique. In this research, carbon dots (CDs) are self-assembled with metal organic framework (MOF) to form the composite of CDs@MOF. Successive proton conduction channels consisting of CDs@MOF and sulfonated groups in SPEEK facilitate to conduct protons in multilayered structures of the prepared composite membranes. Additionally, CDs@MOF can combine phosphoric acid (PA) molecules deriving from the formed intermolecular hydrogen bonding. The proton conductivity is further improved because of the multilayered structures reducing the proton conduction resistance. Specifically, the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane exhibits the maximum proton conductivity of (5.02 ± 0.64) × 10−2 S/cm at 160 °C. Notably, the proton conductivity can retain 1.53 × 10−2 S/cm at 80 °C after a 200 h non-stop test. The open circuit voltage peak and power density of a single fuel cell based on the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane respectively reach 0.95 V, 258.2 mW/cm2 at 100 °C and 0.96 V, 369.9 mW/cm2 at 120 °C.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (A), (B), (C) and SEM images (D), (E), (F) of carbon dots, metal organic framework, carbon dots@metal organic framework.
Schematic preparation processes and the multilayered structures of the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 membranes.
Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (A) and X-ray diffraction of the prepared (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 composite membranes and the components.

Thermal stability (A), (B), oxidative stability (C), component stability (D)–(F) and mechanical property (G)–(I) of the prepared (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 composite membranes. For oxidative stability, mass and volume variations of the composite membranes immersing in Fenton reagent at 68 °C for 17 h. For component stability, mass variation of the composite membranes with humidification at 100 °C for 200 h. For mechanical property, the pristine membranes and the membrane samples after the conductivity stability test at 80 °C for 200 h were stretched under a stretching speed of 5 mm/min (H).
Weight gain (A), dimension swelling (B), (C), density increase (D) and water contact angle (E) of the prepared (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3/PA composite membranes.

AFM and SEM images observation on microstructures of the prepared (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes and PA doped composite membranes. The surface observation by AFM and SEM images: (A) and (B) (SPEEK/25%CDs@MOF)3; (C) and (D) (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3; (E) and (F) (SPEEK/50%CDs@MOF)3; The cross-section observation by SEM images: (G) (SPEEK/25%CDs@MOF)3; (H) (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3; (I) (SPEEK/50%CDs@MOF)3. The membrane surface and cross-section of PA doped composite membranes after proton conductivity test at 80 °C in a 200 h non-stop test: (J) and (M) (SPEEK/25%CDs@MOF)3/PA; (K) and (N) (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA; (L) and (O) (SPEEK/50%CDs@MOF)3/PA.

Proton conductivities of (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3/PA composite membranes. Proton conductivities and Arrhenius plots in the range of 80–160 °C: (A) and (B) the pristine PA doped composite membranes, (C) and (D) the membranes after the 200 h non-stop proton conductivity stability test at 80 °C; (E) all PA doped composite membranes at 80 °C in a 200 h non-stop test; (F) (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA at various temperatures in a 52 h non-stop test.
Diagrammatic sketch of the proposed proton conduction process in the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane.
Methanol permeability and the selectivity of the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 membranes during a 168 h non-stop test and the comparison with the reported data.

Single fuel cell performance of the membrane electrode assemblies based on the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3/PA (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes fueling with anhydrous H2/O2 at 100 °C (A) and 120 °C (B), the Pt loading of the electrodes is 0.45 mg/cm2.

head.ce:title: Preparation of high temperature proton exchange membranes with multilayered structures through alternate deposition of carbon dots@Metal organic framework and Sulfonated Poly(Ether Ketone)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High temperature proton exchange membranes (HTPEMs) with multilayered structures based on carbon dots@metal organic framework (CDs@MOF) and Sulfonated Poly(Ether Ketone) (SPEEK) have been prepared with the spin coating technique. In this research, carbon dots (CDs) are self-assembled with metal organic framework (MOF) to form the composite of CDs@MOF. Successive proton conduction channels consisting of CDs@MOF and sulfonated groups in SPEEK facilitate to conduct protons in multilayered structures of the prepared composite membranes. Additionally, CDs@MOF can combine phosphoric acid (PA) molecules deriving from the formed intermolecular hydrogen bonding. The proton conductivity is further improved because of the multilayered structures reducing the proton conduction resistance. Specifically, the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane exhibits the maximum proton conductivity of (5.02 ± 0.64) × 10−2 S/cm at 160 °C. Notably, the proton conductivity can retain 1.53 × 10−2 S/cm at 80 °C after a 200 h non-stop test. The open circuit voltage peak and power density of a single fuel cell based on the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane respectively reach 0.95 V, 258.2 mW/cm2 at 100 °C and 0.96 V, 369.9 mW/cm2 at 120 °C.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive consumption of fossil fuels has caused the increasing energy crisis, climate warming and severe atmospheric pollution, etc [1]. The proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) as one of the alternative energy conversion devices possesses the intrinsic merits of pollution-free, high power density and fast start-up [2]. The PEMFC is thus believed to be a promising candidate to replace fossil fuel driving equipments. In the PEMFC, proton exchange membranes (PEMs) conduct protons in the redox reaction and separate cathode with anode [3,4]. Although the perfluorinated polymer membranes as PEMs have achieved tremendous success, the issues of the proton conductivity decline at temperature exceeding 100 °C and high price limited the wide application [5]. Therefore, the continuous studies on designing functional polymers and synthesizing novel proton conduction carries have been performed to prepare high temperature PEMs (HTPEMs) [6–8].

Metal organic framework (MOF) as a new type of crystalline porous materials exhibits huge potentials as proton conduction carriers because of structure designability and pore size tunability [9]. Chen BL and co-workers reported the structurally flexible MOF with high-density sulfonated groups arranging on channel surfaces for proton conduction [10]. Subsequently, a biomolecule/MOF approach to develop hybrid proton-conductive membranes based on DNA molecules penetrating DNA@ZIF-8 has been reported by Peng SX and co-workers [11]. The water molecules inside the cavities of the ZIF-8 attributed to the improved proton conductivity and the reduced methanol permeation. Wu PY and co-workers fabricated the PEMs through introducing the synthesized composite MOF@GO into the Nafion® membrane [12]. High proton conductivity and low methanol permeation were resulted from a synergetic effect of MOF and graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets. The similar phenomenon has been reported in MOF-808@GO, SO4-MOF-808@GO [13] and the water-stable usCNT@CuTCPP-1.5-M nanosheets membrane [14]. Recently, Chi WS and co-workers prepared the PEMs based on the designed sulfonated MOF [15] and hollow heteropoly acid-functionalized MOF [16]. The introduced MOF has been proven to enhance the internal surface area for proton conduction and improve the mechanical property through reducing the polymer chain swelling.

Additionally, the imidazolium-based MOF has been synthesized and the resulted PEMs possessed high proton conductivity. Specifically, Lan YQ and co-workers reported the in-situ preparation of the Im@Fe-MOF through encapsulating free imidazole molecules into the Fe-MOF with the maximum proton conductivity of 1.21 × 10−2 S/cm at 60 °C [17]. Wang Z and co-workers prepared the PEMs based on imidazole-MOF-801 with long-term stable high proton conductivity, such as 1.28 × 10−1 S/cm under 100 % relative humidity (RH) at 90 °C of C-SPAEKS/Im-MOF-801 [18] and 8.09 × 10−2 S/cm at 80 °C of S–C-SPAEKS/0.5 % Im-MOF-801 [19]. Zeng L and co-workers designed MOF-808-IMC and MOF-808-IMDC with proton conductivities of 5.04 × 10−4 S/cm and 1.11 × 10−2 S/cm at 80 °C under 98 % RH relative to 4.21 × 10−5 S/cm of the original MOF-808 [20]. Additionally, imidazolium ionic liquids (ILs) were used to functionalize the MOF by replacing the formates coordinated with Zr [21,22]. Notably, ILs can be loaded into the pores of MOF to form effective proton transport pathways in well-defined channels without ILs leaking. Tang BB and co-workers introduced the polydopamine modified hollow MOF into Nafion® matix [23]. The working temperature of the DHZIF-8/RN-3 membrane was increased to 120 °C with the anhydrous proton conductivity of 3.66 mS/cm.

Recently, the research on anhydrous proton conductors based on MOF has been performed and the MOF-based HTPEMs have been synthesized in succession. The initial system was originated from the sythesized MOF of β-PCMOF2 at 2009 by Shimizu GKH and co-workers [24], which can conduct protons in regular one-dimensional pores lined with sulfonated groups. Xu TW and co-workers immobilized the N-(3-aminopropyl)-imidazole through MOF of Fe-MIL-101-NH2 in membranes with the convenient pathway for proton mobility [25]. The proton conductivity of 0.04 S/cm at 160 °C surpassed the one of the pure MOF-based membrane. Wan CQ and co-workers synthesized the proton conductive ILs@MOF with long-term durability even at 150 °C [26]. Wang S and co-workers reported that protons were disordered within an inherent hydrogen-bonded infinite chain of acid-base pairs in the MOF with the proton conductivity of 1.45 × 10−3 S/cm at 180 °C [27]. Subsequently, Jana T and co-workers introduced the MOF of UiO-66-NH2 into aryl ether-type polybenzimidazole (PBI) to fabricate the PSM 2–10 % membrane with the proton conductivity of 0.308 S/cm at 160 °C under anhydrous environment [28]. Similarly, Wang L and co-workers introduced the MOF into the crosslinked branched PBI with the proton conductivities of 0.135 S/cm at 160 °C for CBOPBI@MOF50%-IL30.

In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared and self-assembled with MOF to form the composite of CDs@MOF. He RH and co-workers have ever reported that CDs can change the microphase separation and benefit proton conduction in the PP-2CD36.2°/10CD93.1° membrane [29]. The similar study of CDs promoting the proton conduction has been reported in the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane [30] and proton-conductive polymer CDs membranes [31]. The well-ordered microstructures have been demonstrated to effectively reduce the proton conduction resistance [32]. However, the content of CDs in the solution casting membranes was limited due to the consistency between organic and inorganic components [29]. In summary, the spin coating technique was used to construct the HTPEMs with multilayered structures based on CDs@MOF and Sulfonated Poly(Ether Ketone) (SPEEK) on the basis of the following considerations: (1) Successive proton conduction channels facilitated to conduct proton in multilayered structures. (2) CDs and MOF in CDs@MOF provided functional groups of –OH and –COOH to form intermolecular hydrogen bonding with PA molecules. (3) The polymer of SPEEK possessed enough thermal stability and interconnected proton conduction channels from sulfonated groups. (4) Spin coating technique was a facile strategy to construct multilayered structures, which contributed to form compact microstructures and reduce the proton conduction resistance.

Poly (ether ketone) (PEEK) was supplied by Jida High Performance Materials Co., Ltd. Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4) and 2-amino-terephthalic acid were purchased from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Formic acid, methanol and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were supplied by Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Ethylenediamine, concentrated sulfuric acid, citric acid and polyphosphoric acid (PPA) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Additionally, 100 wt% PA was prepared through dissolving 125.2 g of PPA into 20 mL of deionized (DI) water.

Synthesization of CDs Carboxylated CDs were synthesized according to the reported method [30,33]. Specifically, citric acid (1.05 g) and ethylenediamine (0.34 mL) were added in DI water (10 mL) to form homogeneous solution under magnetic stirring. After reacting at 250 °C for 5 h in a reaction kettle, the dark red solution was purified through dialysis for 3 days. The deposit of CDs was obtained after freeze-drying at 25 °C for 12 h.

Synthesization of MOF The MOF of UiO-66-NH2 was synthesized with the reported process [34]. Specifically, ZrCl4 (0.466 g) and 2-amino-terephthalic acid (0.363 g) were respectively dissolved in DMF (50 mL). Formic acid (13.5 mL) was then added dropwise under magnetic stirring for 1 h. The homogeneous solution was transferred to a stainless steel Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. After centrifugation at 10000 revolutions per minute (rpm) for 10 min, the precipitates were respectively washed with DMF and methanol for three times. The MOF of yellowish powder was synthesized after the precipitates were dried in an oven at 60 °C overnight.

CDs (0.0143 g) and UiO-66-NH2 (0.0857 g) were respectively added in DMF (10 mL). The brown CDs@MOF/DMF homogenous solution was prepared after ultrasonic oscillation for 30 min and magnetic stirring for 12 h.

The polymer of SPEEK with a sulfonation degree (SD) of 76.6 % was prepared through the sulfonation of PEEK with concentrated sulfuric acid [35]. The SPEEK/DMF (25 wt%) solution was prepared through dissolving SPEEK into DMF under magnetic stirring at 60 °C for 2 h. As discussed previously [36], the thickness of each layer in the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 membranes was controlled by optimizing solution concentration and adjusting spin coating duration. Specifically, a cleaned glass plate was covered with 2 mL of SPEEK/DMF solution and spin coated at 800 rpm for 10 s. A thin SPEEK layer was formed after the glass plate heating at 60 °C for 5 min. Subsequently, 2 mL of CDs@MOF/DMF solution was dropped on the SPEEK monolayer and dried at 60 °C for 20 min. The (SPEEK/25%CDs@MOF)3 membrane was prepared repeating the processes for 3 times. Similarly, (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3 and (SPEEK/50%CDs@MOF)3 membranes were respectively prepared with each 4 mL and 6 mL of CDs@MOF/DMF solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PA doped composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The pristine (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes were immersed in 100 wt% PA in closed containers at room temperature for 12 h to prepare PA doped composite membranes, such as (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3/PA (x: 25, 40, 50).

Various measurements have been performed, including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), component stability, oxidative stability, mechanical strength, scanning electron microscope (SEM), atomic force microscope (AFM), weight gain, dimension swelling, water contact angle (WCA), proton conductivity, proton conductivity stability, methanol permeability and single fuel cell performance. More details about test processes and relevant equipments were provided in the Supplementary Information (SI) file.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesization of CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF
ce:section.ce:para: The morologies of CDs, MOF and CDs@MOF were visually observed by TEM images and SEM images. The TEM, SEM images showed the spherical CDs with a diameter of (92.5 ± 10.8) nm (Fig. 1 (A)–(D)) and square frustum shaped MOF with the size of (172 ± 15.8) nm (Fig. 1(B)–(E)). The particle sizes from the SEM and TEM images were determined by the software of Nano Measurer. The self-assembly of CDs with MOF was determined by the TEM image (Fig. 1 (C)). Although it is hard to distinguish CDs and MOF in the SEM image (Fig. 1 (F)), the element dispersion (Figs. S1–S3) can support the compact combination of CDs and MOF in CDs@MOF.

Fig. 2 schematically represented the preparation processes of the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 membranes, including spin coating of the SPEEK/DMF solution and drying CDs@MOF/DMF solution. The forces of interfacial tension and intermolecular hydrogen bonds were the primary force to drive the self-assembly of CDs with MOF to form stable CDs@MOF. Additionally, the formed intermolecular hydrogen bonds between CDs@MOF and sulfonated groups of SPEEK ensured the compact multilayered structures in the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 membranes. The thickness values of a monolayer SPEEK layer and a thin CDs@MOF layer were respectively (22.8 ± 2.5) μm and (17.2 ± 1.7) μm.

The FTIR spectra in Fig. 3 (A) exhibited the CDs@MOF with the characteristic peaks at 3406 cm−1, 1707 cm−1 and 1482 cm−1 corresponding to –OH, –COOH in CDs and –NH2 in MOF. Some small peaks at approximate 1182 cm−1, 1078 cm−1 and 1023 cm−1 were assigned to the vibration of OSO groups in SPEEK [37]. Additionally, the mentioned characteristic peaks from CDs@MOF and SPEEK were observed in the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 membranes, albeit with the overlap of some peaks. The results meant that the components could maintain stable structures stability without degradation during the preparation process of the composite membranes.

The XRD profiles in Fig. 3 (B) provided the information to understand the overall microstructures of CDs@MOF and composite membranes. The MOF and CDs@MOF exhibited sharp peaks at 2θ = 7.5°. The result meant that the MOF kept the crystal structure even after the self-assembly with CDs. A board peak centering at 2θ = 20.0° was indicative of the CDs with non-crystalline structure, which was coincided with the SPEEK/CQD/g-C3N4 membrane reported by Entezari MH and co-workers [38]. The (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3 membrane presented a board peak centering at 2θ = 25.5°, deriving from the amorphous microstructure of SPEEK polymer. Notably, the main peak moved to 2θ = 22.3° in the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane. According to Bragg's equation, a lower shift of 2θ values was indicative of the skeleton of the membranes swelling, deriving from PA molecules occupying the gaps between layers [39]. Additionally, the lower content of MOF resulted in the absence of the sharp peak from CDs@MOF in the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3 and (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membranes.

The thermal degradation behaviors of the prepared composite membranes were investigated with TGA curves. Fig. 4 (A) exhibited the CDs@MOF with an initial mass loss period at 180 °C, deriving from the decomposition of MOF at 155 °C and CDs at 165 °C. The improvement on thermal decomposition temperature was primarily caused by the interaction between MOF and CDs. From Fig. 4 (B), the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes showed similar TGA curves, including the individual decomposition of CDs@MOF at 180 °C and SPEEK polymer at 305 °C. For the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane, the continuous mass loss was a result of the dehydration of PA molecules.

The variations on mass and morphology of composite membranes immersing in Fenton reagent were recorded to evaluate the chemical stability. Notably, the pristine membranes rather than PA doped composite membranes were used without the concern of the PA molecules leaking. From Fig. 4 (C), the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes presented the residual mass percents of 64.9 %, 50.2 % and 64.0 %. From the insert images, all membrane samples maintained complete appearance without obvious cracks. The degradation of SPEEK polymer led to the mass loss of composite membranes. Hang QH and co-workers reported that the SPEEK membrane would be degraded in Fenton reagent at 60 °C for 12.3 min [40]. The strategies of the crosslinked SPEEK [41], the introduction of free radical scavengers [42] or metal protectants [43] could effectively reinforce the oxidative stability of the SPEEK-based composite membranes.

The mass variation of composite membranes in a closed oven at 100 °C with full humidification was measured to assess the component stability. From Fig. 4(D)–(F), the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes possessed the residual mass percents of 92.3 %, 90.1 %, 87.6 %, which were respectively higher than 70.2 %, 55.9 %, 71.1 % of the corresponding PA doped composite membranes. Additionally, the quick leakage of PA molecules occurred in the initial period, which was coincided with the research in the PBI/SPAEK-SPOSS-1% membrane [44].

For PEMs, the improvement on proton conductivity was usually achieved at the expense of sacrificing stiffness, especially in the long-term operation of the PEMFC. The tensile stress (E) and stain (ε) of the prepared composite membranes were measured to characterize the mechanical property. From Fig. 4 (G), the E values were respectively (12.9 ± 1.75) MPa of (SPEEK/40%MOF)3, (14.2 ± 2.30) MPa of (SPEEK/40%CDs)3 and (7.71 ± 0.18)-(19.4 ± 3.26) MPa of the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes. From our viewpoint, the inorganic CDs@MOF facilitated to enhance the stiffness of composite membranes relative to organic proton conduction carriers. For PA doped composite membranes, the breakdown of van der Waals force was the primary reason to the degradation of polymer molecular chains, further reduced the stiffness, such as (3.34 ± 1.03)-(8.60 ± 1.21) MPa of the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3/PA (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes (Fig. 4 (H)). Most importantly, the E of 9.87 MPa was retained with the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane after the proton conductivity stability test at 80 °C for 200 h (Fig. 4 (I)). As regards to ε values, the doped PA molecules inevitably swelled the polymer skeleton and thus caused the increase from (12.3 ± 1.89)-(22.3 ± 2.75)% of (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 to (47.7 ± 1.61)-(184 ± 3.88)% of (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3/PA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Weight gain, dimension swelling and water contact angle
ce:section.ce:para: The content of PA molecules was a key factor to dominate proton conduction processes and mechanical property of PEMs, further affected the performance of the PEMFC. The weight gain (WG), thickness swelling (TS), volume swelling (VS) and density increase (DI) were measured with Eqs. S3-S7. From Fig. 5 (A) , the WG value of (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA was (138 ± 12.2)%, higher than (33.0 ± 2.75)% of (SPEEK/25%CDs@MOF)3/PA and (27.3 ± 4.61)% of (SPEEK/50%CDs@MOF)3/PA. The result meant that the excessive CDs@MOF hindered the combination of PA molecules due to the aggregation of CDs@MOF. Additionally, the synergistic effect of CDs and MOF was determined according to the WG values, such as (23.2 ± 6.24)% of (SPEEK/40%CDs)3/PA and (25.8 ± 3.35)% of (SPEEK/40%MOF)3/PA. Correspondingly, the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane possessed the maximum TS of (32.8 ± 4.45)% (Fig. 5 (B)) and VS of (37.8 ± 5.372)% (Fig. 5 (C)). The similar TS and VS values indicated that PA molecules were accommodated in the gaps between the SPEEK layer and the CDs@MOF layer. Therefore, the DI value of (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA reached (73.4 ± 16.0)% (Fig. 5 (D)).

The WCA was measured to evaluate the wettability variation with PA molecules. Yoo DJ believed that the surface wettability of the PEMs and the bipolar plate affected the output of the PEMFC [45]. From Fig. 5 (E), the WCA values were 63.6° of (SPEEK/25%CDs@MOF)3, 59.2° of (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3 and 61.9° of (SPEEK/50%CDs@MOF)3. The hydrophilic surface was primarily derived from the sulfonated groups in SPEEK polymer. The hydrophilicity was further enhanced with the doped PA molecules, which was reflected in the reduced WCA values, such as 42.7° of (SPEEK/25%CDs@MOF)3/PA, 37.7° of (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA and 41.9° of (SPEEK/50%CDs@MOF)3/PA. From our perspective, the doped PA molecules were well dispersed in the inner gaps between layers and the surface of the membranes.

The morphologies of the prepared membranes were examined by AFM and SEM images. From the AFM images in Fig. 6 (A)–(C), some aggregations of irregular rectangles were observed in the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes. Notably, smaller aggregations of CDs@MOF in (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3 were formed and linearly dispersed. Additionally, the visual observation in larger scale on the surface with SEM images (Fig. 6(D)–(F)) was well coincided with the AFM images observation. The element dispersion in the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes (Figs. S4–S6) supported the excessive accumulation of CDs@MOF on the surface of the (SPEEK/50%CDs@MOF)3 membrane. Furthermore, the prepared composite membranes from the spin coating method exhibited the multilayered structures with good compactness on cross-section (Fig. 6(G)–(I)). For PA doped composite membranes, the microstructures on surface and cross-section were observed with SEM images (Fig. 6(J)–(L) and (Fig. 6(M)–(O))) while they underwent the proton conductivity stability test at 80 °C for 200 h. Notably, the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane possessed the interconnected three-dimensional network microstructure (Fig. 6 (K)), which facilitated to conduct protons. Additionally, the compact microstructures on cross-section guaranteed the long-term operation of the prepared PA doped composite membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proton conductivity and proton conductivity stability
ce:section.ce:para: For PA doped composite membranes, the temperature-dependent proton conductivity (σ) values were test under anhydrous conditions in the range of 80–160 °C. From Fig. 7 (A) , all membranes exhibited the increased σ values with temperature rising. Notably, the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane possessed the maximum σ values, such as (1.73 ± 0.60) × 10−2 S/cm at 80 °C and (5.02 ± 0.64) × 10−2 S/cm at 160 °C. The results were comparable with 7.0 × 10−2 S/cm at 160 °C of AmPBI-PIL-5-MXene-3/PA [46], lower than 8.9 × 10−2 S/cm at 200 °C of 10%PAF-6-PA/OPBI [47], higher than 3.7 × 10−2 S/cm at 160 °C of TaPBI-PSBI(40) [48] and 4.8 × 10−2 S/cm at 150 °C of PES-PVP [49]. From our perspective, the enhanced σ values were primarily derived from higher content of PA molecules and the reduced proton conduction resistance. Additionally, the σ values reached 1.29 × 10−2 S/cm, 1.62 × 10−2 S/cm and 1.43 × 10−2 S/cm at 160 °C while the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3/PA (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes underwent the non-stop proton conductivity stability test at 80 °C for 200 h (Fig. 7 (C)). Notably, there was a slight fluctuation of σ values due to the leakage of PA molecules during the test process.

The energy activation (Ea) was frequently used to assess the proton conduction resistance of PEMs. From Fig. 7 (B) and (D), all PA doped composite membranes exhibited the linear Arrhenius behaviors. The Ea values were calculated with Arrhenius Equation (Eq. S(9)). From Table S1, the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane possessed the minimum Ea value of 16.4 kJ/mol, which was increased to 28.8 kJ/mol after the proton conductivity stability test. The similar phenomenon has also been respectively observed in (SPEEK/25%CDs@MOF)3/PA with 18.5 kJ/mol, 20.7 kJ/mol and (SPEEK/50%CDs@MOF)3/PA with 20.4 kJ/mol, 38.4 kJ/mol. The reported Ea values were 13.00 kJ/mol of C-SPAEKS/Im@MOF-801-4% [50], 19.62 kJ/mol of (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA [30] and 17.81 kJ/mol of ZIF-8/PBI-1 [51]. The results meant that the proton conduction resistance was increased after the proton conductivity stability test, which was mainly caused by the leakage of PA molecules during the test process. Additionally, the synergistic effect of CDs with MOF on structure stability of composite membranes has been confirmed because the proton conductivity variation was positively correlated with temperature rising.

Besides the long-term proton conductivity of the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3/PA (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes, the ones of the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane in the range of 90–120 °C were respectively measured to further understand proton conductivity stability. From Fig. 7 (E), the σ values of the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3/PA (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes respectively reached 0.22 × 10−2 S/cm, 1.53 × 10−2 S/cm and 0.38 × 10−2 S/cm while there were a gradual decrease on σ values in a 200 h non-stop test. From our perspective, the decrease was primarily resulted by the leakage of PA molecules during the test process [36]. Additionally, the σ values of the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane were respectively stable at 5.62 × 10−3 S/cm at 90 °C and 4.58 × 10−3 S/cm at 100 °C for 52 h, 3.52 × 10−3 S/cm at 110 °C for 30 h, 6.72 × 10−3 S/cm at 120 °C for 32 h (Fig. 7 (F)). The dimension alteration of the membrane samples contacting with platinum wire electrode brought out the decrease on σ values [52].

The proposed proton conduction process in the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane was schematically described in Fig. 8 . Undoubtedly, successive PA molecular chains dominated the proton conduction with the matrix transport process obeying the Vehicular mechanism. The multilayered structures with the well-ordered components dispersion can effectively reduce the proton conduction resistance [52]. Additionally, –OH group of CDs and –NH2 group of MOF in CDs@MOF as well as sulfonated groups of SPEEK constituted proton conduction channels, which further promoted proton conduction in the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane. Yu DM and co-workers reported that sulfonated groups constituting proton conduction channels would endow polytetrafluoroethylene with the ability to conduct protons [53]. He RH and co-workers believed that the CDs brought on the property changes of composite membranes through the formation of continuous channels, which would further benefit proton conduction [29]. Recently, Pei SP and co-workers designed a highly conductive Ce-based MOF and demonstrated the enhanced chemical durability of membranes via the radical quencher [54].

The methanol permeability (p) values were calculated with Eq. S(10), such as (0.98–1.42) × 10−6 cm/s2, (2.11–2.46) × 10−6 cm2/s and (1.68–2.01) × 10−6 cm2/s of (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes during a 168 h non-stop test. From Fig. 9 , the p values were comparable with the reported SPEEK-based PEMs [55-60]. Correspondingly, the selectivity (β) values defining as the ratio of conductivity to methanol permeability were respectively (1.72–2.49) × 104 S s/cm3, (2.04–2.38) × 104 S s/cm3 and (1.69–2.02) × 104 S s/cm3. Relative to reported β values [55–60], the further improvement on β values of the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes could be achieved by reducing the p values. The subsequent study would focus on enhancing compactness through adjusting the proportion of components.

The membrane electrode assemblies (MEAs) based on the (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3/PA (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes have been fabricated for single fuel cell tests. All MEAs possessed similar thickness with the same preparation process, expecting to eliminate the effects of factors other than membrane properties [61]. Fig. 10 (A) and (B) respectively exhibited the voltage-current density curves, power density-current density curves at 100 °C and 120 °C. Specifically, the (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane exhibited the open circuit voltage (OCV) of 0.95 V, the peak power density (PPD) of 258.2 mW/cm2 at 100 °C and 0.96 V, 369.9 mW/cm2 at 120 °C. Higher OCV was primarily derived from microstructure compactness of membranes [62]. The PPD value at 120 °C was higher than 165.5 mW/cm2 of PP-2CD36.2/10CD93.1° at 150 °C [29], comparable with 250.8 mW/cm2 of SP-7.5%F at 80 °C [63], lower than 705 mW/cm2 of F6PBI-5% at 160 °C [64] and 550.9 mW/cm2 of AmPBI-CTDTr at 160 °C [65]. Additionally, the single fuel cell performance of the (SPEEK/25%CDs@MOF)3/PA membrane was confined by lower proton conductivity. The subsequent work would focus on reducing the thickness without sacrificing strength of membranes, which was favorable to reduce the internal resistance and even increase the energy density of fuel cells [66]. From our perspective, the fuel cell performance in this study demonstrated that the PEMs based on CDs@MOF with multilayered structures possessed acceptable OCV and desired PPD performance.

High temperature proton exchange membranes were successfully prepared through alternate deposition of carbon dots@metal organic framework as proton conduction carriers and the polymer of sulfonated poly (ether ketone). Carbon dots were self-assembled with metal organic framework with the formation of carbon dots@metal organic framework. The target of quick proton conduction was achieved because carbon dots@metal organic framework and the sulfonated groups in SPEEK constituted successive proton conduction channels. Additionally, phosphoric acid molecules were combined by carbon dots@metal organic framework further promoted proton conduction in the prepared (SPEEK/x%CDs@MOF)3 (x: 25, 40, 50) membranes. The PEMFC equipped with the preferred membrane of (SPEEK/40%CDs@MOF)3/PA exhibited the open circuit voltage of 0.95 V, the peak power density of 258.2 mW/cm2 at 100 °C and 0.96 V, 369.9 mW/cm2 at 120 °C. The research demonstrated that the prepared composite membranes based on carbon dots@metal organic framework were the promising candidates as high temperature proton exchange membranes.

Qingquan Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Weimin Gao: Investigation, Data curation. Niuniu Zhang: Investigation. Xinna Gao: Investigation. Dan Wu: Validation. Quantong Che: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
